turns.text: Well, I take it then, you must get down to this really because you seem to concede the general rule would be that if this is a binding rule of some kind, it really ought to apply to pending cases.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: I think so.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: And so, your real last resort is whether it's really binding on it, on a local agency, whether this kind of circular really determines -- is supposed to determine your conduct.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: And you suggest that you can -- may depart from it now, although you're complying with it now, you can depart from it without violating any kind of Federal Law.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: I think so. I think that because you'd have to be very careful about reviewing the content of the annual contributions contract, which we have done and can find nothing in it that gives HUD the prerogative under the terms of that contract to say what kind of lease we're going to have, whether it should be for six months, one month, one year, or anything like that and it doesn't give them any prerogative to say that you must have an administrative hearing in advance of --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Isn't that the real issue between you and Mr. Nabris?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: That's correct. It's whether the constitution of this country requires that there'd be an administrative hearing or administrative giving of reasons before the eviction proceedings --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: Before you go to Court.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: Before we go to Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. could I follow up one question? Assuming that your view is deceptive on that problem, what -- I'm not quite sure that I understand your view as to what would be your position if, in an administrative -- in any summary eviction proceedings that I cannot be evicted because I have received no reasons for my eviction period, what would happen on that part?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: I would say that would not be a sufficient defense on the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Not a sufficient defense?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: That's right. I cannot be evicted because I received no reason.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right, and then, suppose that she said “I want to prove my answer to the questions of the majority that I was evicted because I was a Negro.”

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: I think she would've been entitled to do so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But she has to make that assertion as a matter of speculation or suspicion, however we feel about her, knew well about her, she cannot explore as to what the reasons have been?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: I think she could. I think she could insist upon her right to examine the agen -- the executive director of the authority. If he got up on the stand and ask “What reason did you have?” He might say “I had no reason at all, I just wanted a vacant apartment there,” but we say, constitutionally, he would be entitled -- that would be a constitutionally acceptable answer and said “I had no reason at all, I just wanted a vacant apartment.”

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And, as Justice White suggested, the language in here must have been that North Carolina was a little overbroad.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: That was the first -- the first opinion was overbroad and I concede that it was, and I don't think that the language accurately portrayed what the Court was thinking and what it was holding. I know it didn't portray what it was actually holding on the basis of the record before it. And then, when it went back on re-hearing, I think it clarified that point.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So, she would get a chance. She would have a right to get the reasons for the eviction?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: A case that much?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Mr. Ed --

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: No, I didn't understand that.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Where?

turns.name: POTTER STEWART
turns.text: You --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Where? In the Courts?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: In the Courts.

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, what if --

turns.name: HUGO BLACK
turns.text: That is the issue between you.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: Well, that has been denied up to now.

turns.name: ABE FORTAS
turns.text: Well, that's right.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: She never -- she has never asked in the Trial Court, if Your Honor please, what the reasons were.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: Now, you're being technical. In North Carolina she's asked.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: No, sir. She did not ask in the trial of this action.

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: She asked in the Supreme Court of North Carolina.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: They should -- There was no evidence, of course, in the Supreme Court, but when an opportunity during the trial --

turns.name: WILLIAM DOUGLAS
turns.text: The tenant -- the point is a point to be decided.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: But when the action was tried, when the matter was before the Trial Court on finding the issues, the Trial Court found on her and she contended that her First Amendment right had been violated. Evidence was introduced on that point. The Court found against her. There was no additional question asked about what the reasons were in that trial of that action.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: On the discover proceedings in these procedures?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: There --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: This procedure and let's see, this is the kind of case that goes --

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: Discover procee --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: The summary eviction proceeding is in what Court?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: Superior Court.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And, in that Court, are there discovery proceedings?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: There are indeed.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: And when she was served, it was possible to have asked the question what was the reason, was there any reason, and so forth?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: She could've had pre-trial discovery by interrogatories, written interrogatories or by pre-trial examinations of --

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Doesn't this start before the Justice of the Peace really?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: Sorry?

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: This starts really before the Justice of the Peace.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: It starts before the Justice of the Peace.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: And then it's --

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: It's de novo.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: And then it's de novo in the Superior Court.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: Completely de novo. It's just like starting all over again. She filed in lieu of pleadings and affidavits setting out what her contentions were about why she was being --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Is there a pre-trial proceeding, as well as pre-trial discovery available under your thrust?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: Pre-trial discovery, yes. But, in an eviction proceeding, the normal course is before a justice and then it goes to --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: So you don't really get to discovery proceedings, I guess.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: You can't.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: You get to the Superior Court?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: Until she appeals from the justice --

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: And then she gets into it, but it's de novo, completely new in the Superior Court and, before you get to that stage, she does have the discovery proceedings available.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: Well I take it that the -- somewhere, I remember reading in here, that the -- one of the officials of the Housing Authority actually appears in Court before the Justice of the Peace.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: And then there was a stipulation about what his testimony would be --

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: In the Superior Court.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: BYRON WHITE
turns.text: And, although they didn't need to sign that stipulation, they could've had him there.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: They could've had him there and they could've cross-examined him and ask him any number of questions, at least as far as this record is concerned they could've, because there was no question that they asked that was denied by the Court, there's no ruling of irrelevancy anywhere along the line by the Trial Court, and they made no exception. They didn't ask the Trial Court, “now, will you expand this hearing in this Trial Court to require into other reasons that might have existed?” They didn't ask that.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: The Court said it was immaterial, didn't it?

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: No, sir. The Trial Court did not. It was never said in the Trial Court that it was immaterial at any stage because the question was never asked in the Trial Court, never. It was only the language, that Your Honor was referring to, appears for the first time in the per curiam deci -- opinion of the Supreme Court of North Carolina. That's the only place that that sentence has ever appeared in this whole lawsuit, from the beginning to the end. That's the one place it's appeared.

turns.name: WILLIAM BRENNAN
turns.text: Well, the sentence at least has created a confusion.

turns.name: DANIEL K. EDWARDS
turns.text: It has created a confusion, yes, sir.

turns.name: EARL WARREN
turns.text: We'll recess now.
